metadata.scope: Definitions and explicitly defined terminology from the textbook sections covered by CO 250 Reviews 1–11.
metadata.authority: The supplied textbook is primary; review sheets determine course order and emphasis.

definitions.title: Optimization problem
definitions.formalStatement: A problem of choosing values for unknown quantities to maximize or minimize an objective while satisfying all stated constraints.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It asks for the best allowable decision, not merely any allowable decision.

definitions.title: Formulation
definitions.formalStatement: A formulation is a mathematical representation of an optimization problem.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It translates the word description into variables, an objective, and constraints.

definitions.title: Decision variable
definitions.formalStatement: A variable is an unknown quantity whose value is chosen by the optimization model.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It represents one of the decisions the model must make.

definitions.title: Objective function
definitions.formalStatement: The objective function assigns a value to each candidate solution and is the quantity to be maximized or minimized.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the score used to decide which feasible solution is best.

definitions.title: Mathematical constraint
definitions.formalStatement: A mathematical constraint is an equation or inequality that every feasible assignment of the variables must satisfy.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Constraints encode limits, balances, requirements, and sign restrictions.

definitions.title: Solution to a formulation
definitions.formalStatement: A solution is an assignment of values to every variable in the formulation.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A solution need not satisfy the constraints; feasibility is a separate condition.

definitions.title: Feasible solution
definitions.formalStatement: A solution is feasible if it satisfies every constraint in the formulation.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is an allowable decision, though not necessarily the best one.

definitions.title: Optimal solution
definitions.formalStatement: An optimal solution is a feasible solution with objective value at least as good as every other feasible solution.
definitions.formalLatex: x^*\in S,\qquad f(x^*)\ge f(x)\ \text{for all }x\in S\quad\text{(maximization)}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is feasible and no competing feasible decision has a better value.

definitions.title: Affine function
definitions.formalStatement: A function from R^n to R is affine when it is a linear function plus a constant.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Variables appear only to the first power and are not multiplied together; a constant term is allowed.

definitions.title: Linear function
definitions.formalStatement: An affine function is linear when its constant term is zero.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Every linear function is affine, but an affine function with a nonzero constant is not linear.

definitions.title: Linear constraint
definitions.formalStatement: After moving variables to the left and constants to the right, a linear constraint has one of three forms with a linear left-hand side.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Its boundary is described by a first-degree expression in the decision variables.

definitions.title: Linear program
definitions.formalStatement: A linear program maximizes or minimizes an affine objective subject to finitely many linear constraints.
definitions.formalLatex: \operatorname{optimize}\; c^{\mathsf T}x+z\qquad\text{subject to finitely many linear constraints}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The objective and every constraint are affine in the variables.

definitions.title: Integer program
definitions.formalStatement: An integer program is a linear program in which a nonempty subset of variables is required to take integer values.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It adds indivisibility requirements to an LP.

definitions.title: Pure integer program
definitions.formalStatement: An integer program is pure when every decision variable is required to be integer.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: There are no continuous decision variables.

definitions.title: Mixed integer program
definitions.formalStatement: An integer program is mixed when some, but not all, decision variables are required to be integer.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Integer and continuous variables appear in the same model.

definitions.title: Binary variable
definitions.formalStatement: A binary variable may take only the values zero and one.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It represents a two-way choice such as off/on or no/yes.

definitions.title: Nonlinear program
definitions.formalStatement: In the textbook convention, a nonlinear program minimizes a function subject to finitely many functional inequalities.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The functions may be nonlinear; an LP is the special case in which they are affine.

definitions.title: Value of a solution
definitions.formalStatement: The value of a candidate vector is the value of the objective function evaluated at that vector.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the score attached to that particular assignment.

definitions.title: Optimal value
definitions.formalStatement: The optimal value is the objective value attained by an optimal solution.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: An LP can have many optimal solutions but only one optimal value.

definitions.title: Feasible LP
definitions.formalStatement: An LP is feasible if it has at least one feasible solution.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Its constraints can all be satisfied simultaneously.

definitions.title: Infeasible LP
definitions.formalStatement: An LP is infeasible if it has no feasible solution.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: No assignment satisfies all constraints at once.

definitions.title: Unbounded LP
definitions.formalStatement: A maximization LP is unbounded when feasible solutions can have arbitrarily large objective value; for minimization, arbitrarily small value.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: There is no finite best objective value in the improving direction.

definitions.title: Certificate of infeasibility
definitions.formalStatement: For the system Ax=b, x≥0, a vector y satisfying y^T A≥0^T and y^T b<0 is a certificate of infeasibility.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A weighted combination of the equations produces a contradiction with nonnegativity.

definitions.title: Certificate of unboundedness
definitions.formalStatement: For max{c^T x:Ax=b,x≥0}, a feasible point x-bar and direction d form a certificate when Ad=0, d≥0, and c^T d>0.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The ray x-bar+td stays feasible and improves forever.

definitions.title: Certificate of optimality
definitions.formalStatement: A certificate of optimality is a verifiable construction of a bound matching the value of a feasible solution.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Feasibility supplies one side of the claim and the matching bound proves no better solution exists.

definitions.title: Standard equality form
definitions.formalStatement: An LP is in standard equality form when it is a maximization problem, every non-sign constraint is an equality, and every variable is nonnegative.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: SEF is the representation used by the course simplex method.

definitions.title: Free variable
definitions.formalStatement: A variable is free when the formulation imposes no sign restriction on it.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It may be negative, zero, or positive and can be replaced by x_j^+−x_j^− with both new variables nonnegative.

definitions.title: Slack variable
definitions.formalStatement: A nonnegative slack variable converts a linear inequality into an equality.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Its value measures unused capacity; for a ≥ constraint it is subtracted.

definitions.title: Column submatrix A_J
definitions.formalStatement: For a set J of column indices, A_J is the matrix made from the columns A_j with j in J, in index order.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It extracts the columns chosen by an index set.

definitions.title: Basis
definitions.formalStatement: Assuming A has linearly independent rows, a set B of column indices is a basis when A_B is square and nonsingular.
definitions.formalLatex: B\text{ is a basis}\iff A_B\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}\text{ and }\det(A_B)\ne0
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The selected columns form an invertible square matrix.

definitions.title: Basic and nonbasic variables
definitions.formalStatement: For a basis B, x_j is basic when j∈B and nonbasic when j belongs to the complementary index set N.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The basis partitions the variables into the ones solved from the equations and the ones set to zero in the basic solution.

definitions.title: Basic solution
definitions.formalStatement: The basic solution associated with B satisfies Ax=b and sets all nonbasic variables to zero.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Each basis determines exactly one solution of the equality system.

definitions.title: Basic feasible solution
definitions.formalStatement: A basic feasible solution is a basic solution that is componentwise nonnegative.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the feasible point associated with a basis; basic variables may still equal zero.

definitions.title: Feasible basis
definitions.formalStatement: A basis is feasible when its associated basic solution is feasible.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Feasibility is a property of the basis through its unique basic solution.

definitions.title: Canonical form for a basis
definitions.formalStatement: An SEF LP is in canonical form for B when the basis columns form the identity and the basic objective coefficients are zero.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The basic variables can be read directly from the right-hand side and do not appear in the reduced objective.

definitions.title: Entering variable
definitions.formalStatement: In a simplex iteration, an entering variable is a selected nonbasic variable with a positive objective coefficient in the current canonical maximization form.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the nonbasic variable simplex attempts to increase from zero.

definitions.title: Leaving variable
definitions.formalStatement: The leaving variable is the basic variable selected by the minimum-ratio test for the entering column.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It reaches zero first as the entering variable increases.

definitions.title: Degenerate simplex iteration
definitions.formalStatement: A simplex iteration is degenerate when the step length is zero, so the basis changes but the basic feasible solution does not.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The algorithm pivots without moving to a new point or improving the objective.

definitions.title: Cycling
definitions.formalStatement: Cycling is the behavior in which simplex revisits bases indefinitely and therefore fails to terminate.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Repeated degenerate pivots can return the algorithm to an earlier basis.

definitions.title: Bland’s rule
definitions.formalStatement: Bland’s rule chooses the smallest-index eligible entering variable and the smallest-index eligible leaving variable among ratio-test ties.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: This deterministic tie-breaking rule prevents cycling.

definitions.title: Simplex ratio test
definitions.formalStatement: For an entering column k in canonical form, the ratio test chooses the largest nonnegative step that preserves nonnegativity by taking the smallest ratio of the current right-hand side to a positive entry in that column.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It identifies which basic variable reaches zero first and therefore which row can leave the basis.

definitions.title: Matrix pivot operation
definitions.formalStatement: For T_ij nonzero, pivoting T on element (i,j) scales row i so the pivot becomes one, then eliminates column j from every other row.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the Gauss-Jordan row operation that updates a tableau from one basis to the next.

definitions.title: Auxiliary variable
definitions.formalStatement: An auxiliary variable is an added nonnegative variable used to create an obvious identity basis for Phase I.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is temporary and must be driven to zero before returning to the original LP.

definitions.title: Auxiliary LP
definitions.formalStatement: After making b nonnegative, the auxiliary LP optimizes the negative sum of auxiliary variables subject to the augmented equalities.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Its optimum is zero exactly when all auxiliary variables can be removed while retaining feasibility.

definitions.title: Phase I
definitions.formalStatement: Phase I solves the auxiliary LP to find a basic feasible solution of the original LP or prove the original LP infeasible.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the feasibility-finding phase of two-phase simplex.

definitions.title: Phase II
definitions.formalStatement: Phase II removes the auxiliary variables, restores the original objective, and optimizes from the feasible basis found in Phase I.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Once feasibility is settled, ordinary simplex solves the original objective.

definitions.title: Feasible region
definitions.formalStatement: The feasible region of an LP is the set of all of its feasible solutions.
definitions.formalLatex: S=\{x:\ x\text{ satisfies every constraint of }(P)\}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the geometric set in which the optimizer is allowed to search.

definitions.title: Euclidean norm
definitions.formalStatement: The Euclidean norm of a vector is the square root of the sum of the squares of its entries.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the ordinary length of the vector and its distance from the origin.

definitions.title: Hyperplane
definitions.formalStatement: For nonzero a, the solution set of one linear equality is a hyperplane.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is an (n−1)-dimensional flat boundary.

definitions.title: Halfspace
definitions.formalStatement: For nonzero a, one side of a hyperplane, including its boundary, is a halfspace.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A single linear inequality describes a halfspace.

definitions.title: Polyhedron
definitions.formalStatement: A polyhedron is the solution set of finitely many linear inequalities, equivalently a finite intersection of halfspaces.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Every LP feasible region is a polyhedron or the empty set.

definitions.title: Line through two points
definitions.formalStatement: The line through x^(1) and x^(2) contains all affine combinations of the two points.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The parameter may extend beyond either endpoint.

definitions.title: Line segment
definitions.formalStatement: The line segment with endpoints x^(1) and x^(2) contains their convex combinations.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It includes both endpoints and every point between them.

definitions.title: Convex set
definitions.formalStatement: A set is convex when it contains the line segment joining every pair of its points.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Moving directly between two points in the set never leaves it.

definitions.title: Properly contained in a line segment
definitions.formalStatement: A point is properly contained in a line segment when it lies on the segment but is distinct from both endpoints.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is an interior point of that segment.

definitions.title: Extreme point
definitions.formalStatement: For a convex set C, x is an extreme point when no line segment contained in C properly contains x.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It cannot be written as a genuine average of two distinct feasible points.

definitions.title: Tight (active) linear constraint
definitions.formalStatement: At a feasible point x-bar, an inequality is tight when it holds with equality.
definitions.formalLatex: a^{\mathsf T}\bar x=\beta\qquad\text{for the constraint }a^{\mathsf T}x\le\beta
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The point lies on the boundary of that constraint's halfspace.

definitions.title: Dual linear program
definitions.formalStatement: The dual interchanges the roles of constraints and variables according to the primal–dual sign and sense table.
definitions.formalLatex: \begin{aligned}(P)&:\max\{c^{\mathsf T}x:Ax=b,\ x\ge0\},\\(D)&:\min\{b^{\mathsf T}y:A^{\mathsf T}y\ge c,\ y\text{ free}\}.\end{aligned}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Each primal constraint creates one dual variable, and each primal variable creates one dual constraint.

definitions.title: Primal–dual pair
definitions.formalStatement: Two LPs related by the dual-construction table are a primal–dual pair; each is the dual of the other.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Dualizing twice returns the original LP.

definitions.title: Complementary slackness conditions
definitions.formalStatement: For a feasible primal–dual pair, each variable is zero or its corresponding opposite-side constraint is tight.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A positive variable can be paired only with a tight corresponding constraint; the OR is inclusive.

definitions.title: Cone generated by vectors
definitions.formalStatement: The cone generated by vectors is the set of all their nonnegative linear combinations.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Coefficients may scale each generator outward but may not reverse it.

definitions.title: Cone of tight constraints
definitions.formalStatement: At x-bar in P={x:Ax≤b}, the cone of tight constraints is generated by the row normals of constraints tight at x-bar.
definitions.formalLatex: J(\bar x)=\{i:\operatorname{row}_i(A)\bar x=b_i\},\qquad C(\bar x)=\operatorname{cone}\{\operatorname{row}_i(A)^{\mathsf T}:i\in J(\bar x)\}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It collects every nonnegative combination of the active constraint normals.

definitions.title: Convex hull
definitions.formalStatement: The convex hull of S is the smallest convex set containing S.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It fills in every convex combination of points in S.

definitions.title: Integer hull
definitions.formalStatement: The integer hull is the convex hull of all integer-feasible points of an integer program.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Optimizing over it gives the IP optimum using a convex feasible region, but describing it may be very hard.

definitions.title: LP relaxation of an IP
definitions.formalStatement: The LP relaxation is the linear program obtained by removing the integrality restrictions from an integer program.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is easier to solve and contains every integer-feasible solution, plus possibly fractional points.

definitions.title: Cutting plane
definitions.formalStatement: For a current fractional LP point x*, a cutting plane is an inequality valid for every IP-feasible point but violated by x*.
definitions.formalLatex: a^{\mathsf T}x\le\beta\;\forall x\in P\cap\mathbb{Z}^n,\qquad a^{\mathsf T}x^*>\beta
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It removes the unwanted fractional point without removing any feasible integer solution.

definitions.title: Relaxation
definitions.formalStatement: For two optimization problems with the same maximization objective over S1 and S2, the second is a relaxation of the first when S2 contains S1.
definitions.formalLatex: S_1\subseteq S_2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (P_2)\text{ is a relaxation of }(P_1)
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A relaxation enlarges the feasible region and therefore gives an upper bound for a maximization problem.

definitions.title: Locally optimal solution
definitions.formalStatement: A feasible point x-bar is locally optimal for a minimization NLP when it is no worse than every feasible point within some positive distance d.
definitions.formalLatex: \exists d>0\;\forall x\in S:\ \lVert x-\bar x\rVert_2<d\implies f(\bar x)\le f(x)
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: No nearby feasible point is better, though a distant one might be.

definitions.title: Globally optimal solution
definitions.formalStatement: A feasible point is globally optimal when no feasible point anywhere has a better objective value.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It solves the full problem, not merely a neighborhood around the point.

definitions.title: Convex function
definitions.formalStatement: A function is convex when its value at every convex combination is at most the same convex combination of endpoint values.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The graph lies below every chord joining two points on the graph.

definitions.title: Epigraph
definitions.formalStatement: The epigraph of a function is the set of points on or above its graph.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A function is convex exactly when its epigraph is a convex set.

definitions.title: Level set
definitions.formalStatement: For a function g and threshold beta, its level set contains the points where g is at most beta.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: A sublevel set of a convex function is convex.

definitions.title: Convex nonlinear program
definitions.formalStatement: An NLP in the textbook minimization form is convex when its objective and every constraint function are convex.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Its feasible region is convex, and local optima are global under this structure.

definitions.title: Tight NLP constraint
definitions.formalStatement: At a feasible point x-bar, the constraint g_j(x)≤0 is tight when g_j(x-bar)=0.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The point lies on the boundary described by that nonlinear inequality.

definitions.title: Subgradient
definitions.formalStatement: For a convex function g, s is a subgradient at x-bar when the affine function through (x-bar,g(x-bar)) with slope s is a global lower bound on g.
definitions.formalLatex: g(\bar x)+s^{\mathsf T}(x-\bar x)\le g(x)\qquad\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^n
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It generalizes a gradient to convex functions that may not be differentiable.

definitions.title: Supporting halfspace
definitions.formalStatement: A halfspace supports a convex set C at x-bar when it contains C and x-bar lies on its boundary hyperplane.
definitions.formalLatex: F=\{x:s^{\mathsf T}x\le\beta\},\qquad C\subseteq F,\qquad s^{\mathsf T}\bar x=\beta
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It touches the set at the chosen point without cutting into the set.

definitions.title: Tight-constraint index set
definitions.formalStatement: J(x-bar) is the set of indices of constraints that are tight at x-bar.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: KKT and geometric optimality use only the active constraint gradients.

definitions.title: Differentiable at a point
definitions.formalStatement: A function is differentiable at x-bar if a vector s gives a first-order linear approximation whose error is negligible relative to the step length.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Near x-bar, the function is approximated by an affine function with slope vector s.

definitions.title: Gradient
definitions.formalStatement: When f is differentiable at x-bar, the unique vector s in its differentiability condition is the gradient of f at x-bar.
definitions.formalLatex: s=\nabla f(\bar x)=\left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}(\bar x),\ldots,\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}(\bar x)\right]^{\mathsf T}
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It collects the first partial derivatives and is the subgradient of a differentiable convex function.

definitions.title: Slater point
definitions.formalStatement: An NLP has a Slater point when there is a point that satisfies every inequality constraint strictly.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is a strictly feasible point and is the regularity assumption used by the course KKT theorem.

definitions.title: Algorithm
definitions.formalStatement: An algorithm is a finite procedure that takes the formulation as input and returns an assignment satisfying the constraints and optimizing the objective when such an assignment exists.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the step-by-step computational method used to solve the mathematical model.

definitions.title: Word description
definitions.formalStatement: The word description is the plain-language statement of the real optimization problem.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is the original problem that the mathematical formulation must represent faithfully.

definitions.title: Solution to the word description
definitions.formalStatement: A solution to the word description is a choice for its unknown quantities; it is feasible when it satisfies every real-world constraint.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Correct formulation requires a correspondence between these real decisions and the formulation's feasible solutions.

definitions.title: Quadratic optimization problem
definitions.formalStatement: A quadratic optimization problem has a quadratic objective and linear constraints.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It is a structured nonlinear program commonly used for squared error, variance, and risk.

definitions.title: Certificate
definitions.formalStatement: A certificate is a compact mathematical object whose stated relations can be checked to verify a claimed optimization outcome.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It turns a solver's answer into a short proof that another person can independently verify.

definitions.title: Equivalent linear programs
definitions.formalStatement: Two LPs are equivalent when infeasibility and unboundedness agree in both directions and an optimal solution of either can be converted into an optimal solution of the other.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Converting to SEF may change the variables and equations without changing the mathematical outcome.

definitions.title: Tableau representation
definitions.formalStatement: A simplex tableau is the augmented matrix that represents both the objective equation and the equality constraints of an SEF LP.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: It stores the current canonical equations in a form suited to pivot operations.

definitions.title: Augmented matrix
definitions.formalStatement: The augmented matrix representing Ax=b is formed by adjoining b as an extra column of A.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: Row operations on this matrix produce equivalent systems of equations.

definitions.title: Slack of a linear constraint
definitions.formalStatement: At a feasible point x, the slack of a ≤ constraint is its right-hand side minus its left-hand side.
definitions.plainLanguageExplanation: The slack is zero exactly when the constraint is tight.
